name: Computer Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 36-course pool is a coherent computer-engineering selection, the ECE hardware spine (circuits ECE 231/232, digital design ECE 251, microprocessors ECE 252, electronics ECE 271, computer systems ECE 353) plus CS 116/CS 332 and upper-level electives in control, power, communications, and computer architecture. But requirementGroups is 0 and no plan of study or credit total parsed, so the required-versus-elective structure and term ordering of the minor cannot be verified from the data. As the general-audience variant it should define a self-contained hardware path for non-ECE majors.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's explicit required-course list and any electives-choose-N rules so its structure is visible rather than implied by a flat course pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph inherited from the major is rich and consistent (explicit grade/compound conditions such as CS 116 'CS 115 with a grade C or better' and ECE 342 '(ECE 231 and ECE 291) or (ECE 231 and ECE 294)'), but its deepestPrereqChain of 8 is very deep for a minor and implies a long ladder of prerequisite courses. The 4 missing references (missingCoursesSample: ECE 291, ECE 394, ECE 414, GER 300) are lab-alternate, project, and GER placeholders.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a compact required subset with a shallower prerequisite depth suited to a minor, and resolve the ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate references so the electives do not point at undefined courses.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the provided data; only a course pool and course descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a short statement of what the Computer Engineering minor certifies (e.g., competence in digital design, microprocessors, and computer architecture) as explicit outcomes.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit total is published (statedTotalCredits is null) and requirementGroups is 0, so creditReconcileDelta is null and the credit load cannot be reconciled or assessed from the data. This is a data-consistency caveat for a minor page rather than proof of imbalance, but a prospective student cannot see how many credits the minor requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's total credit requirement and the per-course credit breakdown so the load is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page provides no parsed plan of study or credit total, and because this variant and the CS-major and EE-major variants present identical course data and metrics in the source, a reader cannot determine the required courses, credits, or how this variant differs from the others.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the minor as an explicit required/elective list with credits, and state precisely which courses are waived or substituted for this audience so the variant is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course pool reflects classical computer-engineering practice (digital design, microprocessors, computer organization/architecture, operating systems) and reaches some current electives (advanced computer architecture ECE 451/452, communications ECE 422/425/481/489). For a general minor it usefully packages the ECE hardware core for students from other disciplines. The same modern gaps as the parent degree apply: no HDL/FPGA, embedded/RTOS, ML-hardware, or hardware-security content.
dimensions.suggestion: Offer at least one modern elective (HDL/FPGA design or an embedded/edge-AI course) within the minor so it certifies contemporary hardware skills, not only classical circuits and microprocessors.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and technically specific (ECE 353 on register-transfer logic and CPU design, ECE 251 on combinational/sequential logic, ECE 489 on Matlab/Simulink and Software-Defined-Radio labs, CS 332 on process management and concurrency). Only minor truncations and the ECE 429 'Pre or' fragment detract.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with grade and compound conditions (e.g., ECE 431 'ECE 333, or ECE 232 and MATH 337', ECE 495 '(ECE 294 and ECE 353) or (ECE 394 and ECE 353)'). Clarity is reduced by the deep (depth-8) chains that are heavy for a minor and by dangling lab-alternate references ECE 291/ECE 394 and project ECE 414.

missingTopics.rationale: The minor exposes a 36-course pool with an 8-deep prerequisite chain but no parsed required list, so a student cannot see the minimal path; a short, shallow required set (e.g., ECE 251, ECE 252, ECE 353) is the first gap to fill.

missingTopics.topic: Hardware description languages and FPGA design
missingTopics.rationale: The pool teaches gate/register-level design (ECE 251/353) but no Verilog/VHDL or FPGA flow; an HDL/FPGA elective would let minors design on programmable hardware.
ECE 252 microprocessors and ECE 395 lab cover bare-metal work, but no embedded/RTOS course is offered in the pool, a core modern CmpE skill.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning and hardware for AI
missingTopics.rationale: With computer-architecture electives (ECE 451/452) available, adding an ML or ML-accelerator elective would give the minor a hardware-for-AI dimension it currently lacks.
No security content accompanies the architecture and microprocessor courses; a hardware-security elective would round out the minor for connected-device work.

emergingTrends.rationale: Running ML on microcontrollers is a defining CmpE direction; an edge-AI elective built on ECE 252/353 would modernize the minor.
Standardization on RISC-V makes an open-core lab (building on ECE 353) a high-value, current addition.
Connected embedded devices dominate hiring; pairing microprocessors with a networking elective targets this trend even within a minor.
Side-channel and secure-boot concerns are now mainstream hardware topics the minor could introduce via a single elective.
Modern systems fuse accelerators on one chip; the advanced-architecture electives (ECE 451/452) are the natural vehicle to reflect this.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's explicit required-course list and electives rules; requirementGroups is 0 so no structure is available in the data.
Publish the minor's total credit requirement and per-course credits (currently no credit total appears in the data).
Define a compact required subset with a shallower prerequisite depth than the current depth-8 chains, which are heavy for a minor.
Resolve the dangling ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate and ECE 414 references so electives point at real catalog entries.
Add at least one modern elective (HDL/FPGA or embedded/edge-AI) so the minor certifies contemporary hardware skills.
